It outlines reporting duties for the Board of Trustees, protects the pension fund from debt seizure, defines the duties of the city attorney in relation to the fund, and ensures that individuals already on the pension roll will continue to receive their pensions under the same terms.
classification.reasoning: The provided statute text details the administration and protection of a Firemen's Pension Fund. It covers reporting requirements, legal protections for the fund, the role of the city attorney, and the continuation of existing pensions. There is no mention of race, nor are there any provisions that implicitly suggest racial discrimination. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law. The document type is 'session_laws' as it is presented as part of the 'LAWS OF KENTUCKY' from 1902, indicating it's an act passed during a legislative session. Confidence is high as the content is clearly administrative and financial, with no apparent racial component.

classification.title: AN ACT to amend and revise an act and amendment thereto, entitled "An Act to authorize the appointment of official stenographic reporters in counties containing a population of seventy-five thousand or over," which became a law July 13, 1893.
classification.summary: This act amends a previous law regarding the appointment of official stenographic reporters in counties with a population of seventy-five thousand or over. It repeals sections one and an amendment to section one of the original act and enacts new provisions for the appointment of these reporters. The judges of the circuit court in each judicial district can appoint a reporter for their court or division, and if the district includes multiple counties, a reporter can be appointed for each county or any counties within the district. These reporters must be skilled and hold office for four years, or until a successor is appointed, unless removed earlier. The act also adds a provision requiring a carbon copy of the transcript of testimony to be filed.
classification.reasoning: The statute concerns the administrative procedures for appointing court reporters in counties with a large population. It does not contain any language that explicitly or implicitly discriminates based on race, nor does it relate to any of the typical categories associated with Jim Crow laws such as segregation, voting rights, or marriage restrictions. Therefore, it is classified as 'no' for Jim Crow. The confidence is high because the subject matter is clearly administrative and unrelated to racial matters. The document type is 'session_laws' as it is an act passed by the General Assembly. The racial indicator is 'none' as there is no mention or implication of race.

classification.title: AN ACT to amend an act entitled, "An Act relating to the Asylums for the Insane, and the Institution for Feeble Minded Children, supported by the State," approved March 10, 1894, concerning asylums.
classification.summary: This act amends a previous law concerning asylums for the insane and the institution for feeble-minded children. Specifically, it modifies the criteria for determining when an insane person is considered a pauper, focusing on their ability to pay for board and support dependents. The amendment clarifies the jury's role in making findings on this subject and the Superintendent's duty to receive the patient based on such findings, while preserving the patient's liability for board if they acquire an estate.
classification.reasoning: The statute concerns the classification of insane individuals as paupers for the purpose of admission to state-supported asylums. It details financial criteria related to paying for board and supporting dependents. There is no mention of race, nor are there any provisions that implicitly or explicitly discriminate based on race. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law. The confidence is high because the text is clear and directly addresses the financial status of individuals seeking asylum, with no racial component.

classification.title: An Act to Amend Section 20 of Article 2 of Chapter 48 of the Acts of 1894, Relating to the Liability of Patients for Asylum Board
classification.summary: This act amends a previous law concerning the financial liability of patients in asylums for their board. It authorizes the Auditor of Public Accounts to sue for and recover the cost of board from patients or their responsible relatives (husband, wife, parent) if they have sufficient estate. The rate is set at $200 per year, and legal proceedings can be initiated to subject their estates to payment. The Auditor can employ counsel for collection, with compensation capped at 25% of the collected amount.
classification.reasoning: The statute concerns the financial responsibility of individuals and their families for the cost of care in asylums. It outlines procedures for collecting these debts. There is no mention of race, nor are there any provisions that implicitly target or discriminate against a specific racial group. The language is neutral and applies to any patient with an estate or responsible relatives with an estate. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law.

classification.title: An Act to amend Section 20 of an Act entitled "An Act to provide for the support of patients in the Eastern and Western Lunatic Asylums of Kentucky."
classification.summary: This act amends a previous law concerning the financial support of patients in Kentucky's lunatic asylums. It authorizes the Auditor of Public Accounts to sue for and recover the cost of board for patients from their estates, or from the estates of their husbands, wives, or parents if sufficient. The statute of limitations for such recovery is tolled until the estate is acquired. The Auditor can employ counsel, with compensation capped at 25% of collected sums.
classification.reasoning: The statute concerns the financial responsibility for the care of patients in lunatic asylums. It outlines procedures for recovering costs from patients' estates or their relatives' estates. There is no mention of race, nor any indication of discriminatory practices based on race. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law. The document type is 'session_laws' as it is presented as an 'Act' from the 'LAWS OF KENTUCKY' in 1902.

classification.title: Amending section twenty-one of article two, chapter forty-eight of the acts of one thousand eight hundred and ninety-four
classification.summary: This statute amends a previous act by changing the reporting requirements for pauper patients in asylums. It mandates that the Auditor of Public Accounts prepare and furnish blanks to asylum superintendents for reporting patient information, including name, age, address, commitment and discharge dates, and details about their committee, parents, or husband if married. It also specifies the role of Commonwealth or county attorneys in gathering and providing this information to the superintendent.

classification.title: An Act to Amend Section 547 of the Kentucky Statutes, Relating to Stockholder Liability
classification.summary: This act amends Section 547 of the Kentucky Statutes concerning the liability of stockholders in corporations. It clarifies that stockholders are liable for the unpaid portion of their stock, with exceptions for specific financial institutions like banks and insurance companies. It also addresses the liability of fiduciaries holding stock and the conditions under which stock transfers do not release existing liabilities.

classification.title: AN ACT to aid in the establishing, protecting and building of levees for the public benefit.
classification.summary: This act grants county judges the power to establish and aid in the construction of levees for public benefit, health, convenience, or welfare. To initiate this process, a petition signed by at least five landowners is required, outlining the levee's necessity, route, and the names of affected landowners and tenants. A bond must also be provided to cover establishment expenses and damages.
classification.reasoning: The statute concerns the establishment and construction of levees for public benefit. It outlines the process involving county judges, petitions from landowners, and the payment of damages. There is no mention of race, nor are there any provisions that implicitly or explicitly discriminate based on race. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law.

one nor more than three years .

Garnishments .
CHAPTER 23 .
AN ACT relating to garnishments .
Be itenactedby the General Assembly of the Commonwealth
of Kentucky :
§ 1. That wages earned out of this State and payable
out of this State shall be exempt from attachment or
garnishment in all cases, where the cause of action arose
out of this State, and it shall be the duty of garnishees
in such cases to plead such exemption unless the defendant
is actually served with process.

CHAPTER 26 .
AN ACT ceding to the Government of the United States jurisdic-
tion over certain lands and appurtenances for the establishment
of a national miliary park at Perryville.
Be it enactedby the General Assembly of the Commonwealth
of Kentucky :
§ 1. That the jurisdictionof this Commonwealth ishere-
by ceded to the United States of America over so much of
any lands, not exceeding three thousand acres, as may be
Cedingjurisdic-selectedin Boyle county in this State, near Perryville,
for the purpose of establishing, maintaining , beautifying
and protecting a National Military Park at the battlefield
of Perryville. But this cession is upon the express condi-
tion that the Commonwealth of Kentucky shall so far re-
tain concurrent jurisdictionwith the United States over
said lands as that all criminal and civilprocesses issued
under the authority of this State may be executed thereon
in like manner as if this act had not been passed .
§ 2. That nothing in this act contained shall interfere
with the jurisdictionof the United States over any matter
or subjects set out in the billnow pending in Congress to
establisha National Military Park at the siteof the battle
of Perryville, or with any laws, rulesor regulationsthat
Congress may adopt for the preservation and protection
of its property and rights on said lands and approaches
thereto and the proper maintenance of good order thereon.
§ 3. That all laws and parts of laws , in conflictwith
this act be , and the same are, hereby repealed.
Approved March 17, 1902 .

classification.summary: This act amends section 3117 of the Kentucky Statutes, concerning the selection of an official newspaper for cities of the second class. The city attorney is tasked with annually selecting a daily newspaper to publish abstracts of general council proceedings, ordinances, resolutions, and notices. The price for such publications is capped at the newspaper's regular advertising rates. The city attorney has the discretion to examine subscription books of competing newspapers to make a final determination. Ordinances or resolutions appropriating less than fifty dollars, or those related to street or public improvements, proposals, or bids, are exempt from publication.

classification.summary: This act amends sections 22 and 23 of Article 11 (Public Schools) of an act governing cities of the second class. It modifies the eligibility and voting qualifications for members of the board of education. Specifically, it removes the phrase "and qualified to vote at any and all elections for members of said board" and inserts "but not eligible or qualified to vote at any election for a member or members of said board." The amended section 22 states that persons eligible for the board of councilmen are eligible for the board of education, and persons qualified to vote for city officers are qualified to vote for board of education members. It also declares women with other qualifications required for males to be eligible for the board.

classification.title: AN ACT to authorize the legal voters of the county to vote a school tax for the purpose of extending the terms of the common schools.
classification.summary: This act allows for a county-wide vote on a school tax to extend the terms of common schools. The tax can be an ad valorem tax (up to 15 cents per $100 of taxable property) or a poll tax (up to 50 cents per male inhabitant over 21), or both, for a period of three years. The process involves a petition from 25% of legal voters who are taxpayers, an order from the county judge, and publication of the election notice.

The category is 'education' as it directly relates to school funding and terms. The document type is 'session_laws' as indicated by the header 'LAWS OF KENTUCKY' and 'CHAPTER 48'.

classification.summary: This act amends existing legislation regarding taxation for common schools, specifically focusing on extending the school term. It outlines procedures for county superintendents to manage salary receipts and settlements. Notably, it includes an exemption clause (§ 11) stating that the provisions of this act do not apply to cities of the first, second, third, or fourth class that maintain separate public school systems, nor to towns or villages with organized graded school systems under general or special charter provisions. It also specifies when taxes collected under this act will become effective and exempts taxpayers from district-level taxes for extending school terms if a county-wide tax is voted.

While Section 11 mentions 'separate and distinct' school systems in certain cities, this phrasing, in the context of 1902 Kentucky legislation, could implicitly refer to racial segregation in education. Without explicit mention of race or a clear discriminatory mechanism targeting a specific race, it is classified as 'no' for Jim Crow. classification.title: AN ACT to provide for an amendment to section 181 of the Constitution of Kentucky.
classification.summary: This act proposes an amendment to Section 181 of the Kentucky Constitution. The amendment would allow the General Assembly to authorize cities and towns to levy taxes for municipal purposes on personal property (tangible and intangible) based on income, licenses, or franchises, as an alternative to ad valorem taxes. However, it specifically prohibits first-class cities from omitting ad valorem taxes on certain utility-related properties (steam, railroad, street railway, ferry, bridge, gas, water, heating, telephone, telegraph, electric light, or electric power companies).
classification.reasoning: The statute concerns an amendment to the Kentucky Constitution regarding municipal taxation powers. It allows for alternative methods of taxation on personal property for cities and towns, such as income or license fees, in lieu of ad valorem taxes. It also includes specific provisions for first-class cities regarding utility company property taxation. There is no mention of race, nor are there any provisions that implicitly target or discriminate against any racial group. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law.

classification.summary: This act establishes a board of examiners to regulate the practice of barbering. It outlines requirements for barbers, including a three-year apprenticeship period, and grants the board powers to set sanitary requirements for barber shops in cities of the first, second, and third class, subject to approval by the State Board of Health.
classification.reasoning: The statute establishes a regulatory board for barbers and sets sanitary standards for barber shops. There is no mention of race or any provisions that would implicitly or explicitly discriminate based on race. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law. The document type is 'session_laws' as it is from the 'LAWS OF KENTUCKY' from 1902, indicating it's an act passed by the legislature. The confidence is high because the text clearly focuses on professional licensing and public health without any racial component.

classification.title: An Act to Regulate the Occupation of Barbers
classification.summary: This act establishes a state board of examiners to regulate the occupation of barbers in cities of the first, second, and third class. It requires existing barbers to register and pay a fee, and new applicants to pass an examination covering age, moral character, health, and training. The board is authorized to hold public examinations and incur necessary expenses.
classification.reasoning: The statute establishes a regulatory board for barbers, requiring examinations and licensing. While it sets standards for practice, it does not contain any explicit or implicit racial classifications or restrictions. The requirements apply to all individuals seeking to practice barbering in specified city classes, regardless of race. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law. The document type is 'session_laws' as indicated by 'LAWS OF KENTUCKY' and the year 1902, which typically refers to acts passed during a legislative session.

classification.title: An Act to Regulate the Practice of Barbers
classification.summary: This act establishes a state board to regulate the practice of barbering. It requires barbers to obtain certificates of registration, renew them annually, and display them publicly. The board has the power to revoke certificates for reasons such as conviction of crime, habitual drunkenness, gross incompetency, unsanitary conditions, or failure to comply with renewal requirements. The act also outlines procedures for notice, hearings, and re-granting of revoked certificates, and grants the board power to summon witnesses. It specifically applies to barbers in cities of the first, second, and third class.
classification.reasoning: The statute outlines regulations for the barbering profession, including licensing, renewal, and grounds for revocation of certificates. It does not contain any explicit or implicit racial classifications or restrictions. The focus is on professional standards and public health within the barbering trade. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law. The document type is 'session_laws' as indicated by 'LAWS OF KENTUCKY' and the year 1902, suggesting it's an act passed during a legislative session.

classification.title: Obstructions in Creeks
classification.summary: This statute prohibits the obstruction of public ditches and creeks by individuals and landowners. It mandates that landowners clear brush, trees, and other debris from creeks running through or adjacent to their property twice a year. The law also establishes a system for county judges to appoint inspectors to identify obstructions, notify landowners, and arrange for removal at the landowner's expense if they fail to comply. Water gates designed to allow passage of water and drift are explicitly excluded as obstructions.
classification.reasoning: The statute addresses the maintenance of public ditches and creeks, focusing on the removal of obstructions by landowners and the appointment of inspectors. There is no mention of race, nor are there any provisions that implicitly or explicitly discriminate based on race. The enforcement mechanisms apply universally to all landowners. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law. The document type is 'session_laws' as indicated by 'LAWS OF KENTUCKY' and the year 1902, which corresponds to a legislative session. The confidence is high because the content is clearly about public works and property maintenance, with no racial component.

classification.title: An Act to Protect Wild Birds, Their Nests and Eggs
classification.summary: This act prohibits the taking or needless destruction of the nests or eggs of wild birds other than game birds within Kentucky. It also prohibits possessing such nests or eggs. Violations are classified as misdemeanors, punishable by a fine, imprisonment, or both. Exceptions are made for individuals holding certificates for scientific purposes, granted by specific officials or incorporated societies of natural history, to persons aged fifteen and above who meet character and financial requirements.
classification.reasoning: The statute concerns the protection of wild birds, their nests, and eggs. It outlines penalties for violations and provides an exception for scientific collection. There is no mention of race, nor are there any provisions that implicitly or explicitly discriminate based on race. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law.

classification.title: An Act to Amend Section 4402 of the Kentucky Statutes
classification.summary: This act amends section 4402 of the Kentucky Statutes, requiring county superintendents to enter into a covenant with sufficient security for the faithful discharge of their duties. The bond must be renewed annually and submitted in duplicate to the county clerk and the Superintendent of Public Instruction. It also amends section 4410 by changing the deadline for the superintendent's official report from September 1st to August 1st.

classification.title: Amending sections of the act concerning county superintendents' salaries and duties.
classification.summary: This statute amends existing laws regarding county superintendents. It details how their salaries are calculated based on pupil enrollment, with specific exclusions for children under city school superintendents in certain classes of cities. It also outlines requirements for the superintendent's services to be verified before salary payment and specifies the payment source. Additionally, it mandates that the county clerk forward a certified copy of the superintendent's settlement to the Superintendent of Public Instruction and outlines procedures for the transfer of funds and property upon a superintendent leaving office, with penalties for failure to comply.

The amendment specifies limits on these additional taxes.

The statute's focus is purely on the administrative and financial aspects of public education infrastructure. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law.

classification.title: LAWS OF KENTUCKY
classification.summary: This statute outlines regulations for school districts in Kentucky, including provisions for borrowing money to repair or erect school houses, levying taxes to cover construction costs and indebtedness, and specifying minimum standards for school house construction, including floor space, ceiling height, windows, heating apparatus, and doors. It also details the required furniture and apparatus for each school house and the responsibilities of the trustees and chairman.
classification.reasoning: The statute discusses the financial and physical requirements for school houses in Kentucky. It details how districts can fund construction and repairs, the minimum standards for building size and amenities, and the responsibilities of school trustees. There is no mention of race, segregation, or any discriminatory practices within the text. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law. The document type is 'session_laws' as it is presented as part of the 'LAWS OF KENTUCKY' from a specific legislative session (1902).

classification.title: Amending sec. 4459 Ky. Stats.
classification.summary: This statute amends Section 4459 of the Kentucky Statutes, detailing the required furniture and apparatus for schoolhouses, including desks, chairs, blackboards, maps, and globes. It also outlines the permissible uses of tax proceeds for school purposes, such as purchasing lots, building, repairing, furnishing, supplying apparatus, liquidating prior debts, extending the school term, and supplementing teacher salaries. The amendment specifies that the purposes for which taxes are levied must be clearly stated in election notices.
classification.reasoning: The provided text is an amendment to existing Kentucky statutes concerning the funding and equipping of common schools. It details the physical requirements for schoolhouses and the financial mechanisms for their upkeep and improvement. There is no mention of race, segregation, or any discriminatory practices within this text. Therefore, it is classified as not a Jim Crow law. The document type is 'session_laws' as it refers to amending existing statutes from a legislative session. The category is 'education' as the statute directly pertains to school operations.

It outlines the process for voting on such taxes and the conditions under which trustees can borrow money.

classification.title: Amending sec. 4510 Ky. Stats.
classification.summary: This statute amends Section 4510 of the Kentucky Statutes, concerning teacher attendance at county institutes. It clarifies that schools will suspend during institutes but this suspension will not reduce the teaching session for the district. It also mandates teacher attendance at institutes, with revocation of certificates for non-compliance unless due to sickness or disability. Furthermore, it prohibits granting teaching certificates to individuals who have not attended a full institute session during the school year, unless an exception for sickness or disability is met.
classification.reasoning: The statute focuses on the administrative requirements for teachers to attend professional development institutes. It outlines attendance policies, certificate issuance, and school session adjustments. There is no mention of race, nor are there any provisions that implicitly or explicitly discriminate based on race. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law. The document type is 'session_laws' as indicated by 'LAWS OF KENTUCKY' and the year '1902'.

classification.title: AN ACT concerning circuit courts having six judges.
classification.summary: This act mandates that in districts with six circuit judges, each judicial office must be voted for separately. The ballots will specify the branch and division for each judge position (e.g., criminal branch, chancery branch first division, common pleas branch second division). The candidate receiving the most legal votes for each specific position will be elected.

It specifies separate voting for each judicial office and how ballots should be marked. There is no mention of race, segregation, or any differential treatment based on race. classification.summary: This act details the organization and operation of circuit courts in Kentucky, specifically those with six judges. It also addresses the election and commissioning of circuit judges and the filling of vacancies.

classification.title: An Act to amend an act entitled "An Act to establish the criminal branch of the circuit court of the county of Jefferson, and to define its jurisdiction and powers," approved March 24, 1894.
classification.summary: This act amends a previous act establishing the criminal branch of the circuit court of Jefferson County. It details the appointment and salary of a commissioner and receiver, the court's general term procedures for making rules and appointments, its control over judgments, and its authority to determine state and municipal taxes in property sale actions. The regular judges, with four concurring, shall appoint a commissioner and receiver who will hold office for four years.

It outlines the appointment of court officials, the establishment of court rules, and procedures for handling judgments and property sales. There is no mention of race, nor are there any provisions that implicitly suggest racial discrimination. Therefore, it is classified as not a Jim Crow law.

CHAPTER 66 .
AN ACT to make it unlawful for a person to fraudulently dispose
of the property of another , and to provide a punishment therefor.
Be itenactedby the General Assembly of the Commonwealth
of Kentucky :
§ 1. That any person who shall sell,dispose of or convert
to his or her own use or the use of another , any money ,
property, or other thing of value without the consent of
the owner thereof, shallbe punished by confinement in the
penitentiary for not less than one nor more than five
years; if the money , property, or other thing of value so
sold, disposed of or converted to his or her own use be of
the value of twenty dollars or more ; or be confined in the
county jailfor not less than one nor more than twelve
months if the value be less than twenty dollars .
Approved March 21 , 1902 .

It specifies the number of members, their terms, political party representation, gender balance, residency, age, compensation (none), bond requirements, and oath of office. It also details the board's inability to incur debt or exceed appropriated funds, with personal liability for members who violate these provisions. The board is granted the power to establish, maintain, and conduct the library, and to adopt rules and regulations for its operation. The library is to be open and free to the public.
classification.reasoning: The statute pertains to the governance and operation of free public libraries in first-class cities. It details the composition of the library board, their responsibilities, and financial constraints. There is no mention of race, segregation, or any discriminatory practices within the text. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law. The document type is 'session_laws' as indicated by 'LAWS OF KENTUCKY' and the year 1902, suggesting it's an act passed during a legislative session. The confidence is high because the text clearly focuses on administrative and financial aspects of public libraries without any racial implications.

classification.title: An Act to Amend and Re-enact Section 189 of the Kentucky Statutes, Relating to Public Libraries
classification.summary: This act amends Kentucky Statutes Section 189 concerning the establishment and maintenance of free public libraries. It outlines the powers of library boards, including agreements with existing libraries, and specifies funding sources such as a percentage of common school taxes and a portion of police court fines and costs. The act also authorizes city and county legislative bodies to accept donations for library support. It emphasizes that libraries shall be strictly non-sectarian and non-partisan.

classification.title: AN ACT to amend section 4291, article 1, chapter 110, of the Kentucky Statutes
classification.summary: This act amends Section 4291 of the Kentucky Statutes concerning the appointment of commissioners to assess damages for road construction. It adds provisions allowing the county judge to immediately open and survey a temporary road or passway in cases of emergency, such as roadbed erosion, while permanent road establishment proceedings are underway. The commissioners will then assess damages for this temporary use, and compensation will be handled similarly to the establishment of new roads. The county judge is also empowered to agree on compensation with landowners and tenants.
classification.reasoning: The statute deals with the process of establishing temporary roads and assessing damages in emergency situations. It does not contain any language that explicitly or implicitly refers to race, nor does it establish separate facilities or differential treatment based on race. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law. The document type is 'session_laws' as indicated by 'LAWS OF KENTUCKY' and 'CHAPTER 72 . AN ACT to amend...'. The confidence is high because the content is clearly about road infrastructure and legal procedures, with no racial component.

classification.title: An Act to amend an act entitled 'An Act to provide for the establishment of public roads and passways in the several counties of this Commonwealth', approved March 10, 1894, and to declare an emergency.
classification.summary: This act allows county judges to immediately open and survey temporary roads or passways in cases of extreme emergency, such as roadbeds washing away due to river overflows. Commissioners would then assess damages for these temporary roads, and compensation would be determined and collected similarly to permanent roads. The act declares an emergency due to the slow process of establishing new roads, which can leave citizens cut off from travel and transportation for months.

classification.summary: This act amends a previous law concerning the extension or reduction of city limits for cities of the fourth class. It outlines the process for such changes, including requirements for resident voter or property owner remonstrance, potential material injury to real estate owners, and judicial review. It also specifies procedures for appeals and the certification of judgments to the city board of council. The act clarifies that it does not interfere with pending litigation under the original act.

It focuses on procedures involving resident voters, property owners, court judgments, and city ordinances. classification.title: AN ACT to amend section 69 of an act, entitled "An Act for the government of cities of the firstclass," approved July 1, 1893.
classification.summary: This act amends a previous law concerning the government of first-class cities in Kentucky. Specifically, it modifies section 69 of an act approved on July 1, 1893 (which is section 2832 of the Kentucky Statutes). The amendment concerns the acceptance of ground laid off and dedicated as a street or alley within territories annexed to the city. It states that such ground will not be considered a public way until the dedication is accepted by a resolution or ordinance of the general council, recommended by the board of public works. The act also outlines the process for the city attorney to institute legal action to close streets or alleys that divide squares or lots within the city limits, requiring owners of abutting ground to be made defendants in such suits.
classification.reasoning: The provided statute text is an amendment to an act concerning the governance of first-class cities in Kentucky. It details procedures for accepting new streets and alleys within annexed territories and for closing existing ones. There is no mention of race, nor are there any provisions that implicitly suggest racial segregation or discrimination. The focus is purely on urban planning and property law. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law.

classification.title: An Act to Amend Section 3567 of the Kentucky Statutes, Relating to Public Ways
classification.summary: This act amends Kentucky Statutes Section 3567 concerning the definition of public ways and the process for closing streets or alleys within a city. It clarifies that a street or alley dedicated by an owner becomes a public way only upon acceptance by the city council. It also outlines the procedure for the city attorney to initiate lawsuits to close streets or alleys, requiring consent from all competent owners of adjacent lots or proof of benefit to the city and no injury to non-consenting parties.
classification.reasoning: The statute deals with the legal procedures for managing public ways (streets and alleys) within a city, specifically regarding their dedication and closure. It outlines the roles of the city attorney, city council, and circuit court, and the requirements for obtaining consent or proving benefit for closing a street or alley. There is no mention of race, nor are there any provisions that implicitly target or discriminate against any racial group. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law.

classification.title: An ACT to amend chapter 110, section 4317, of the Kentucky Statutes, entitled "Roads and Passways."
classification.summary: This act amends section 4317 of the Kentucky Statutes concerning "Roads and Passways." It adds provisions for the penalty for a supervisor failing to perform their duties, specifying that such failure is a misdemeanor punishable by a fine of not less than ten nor more than one hundred dollars. The penalty can be recovered by indictment in the circuit court or by warrant issued by the county judge. The supervisor is entitled to a jury trial if demanded.

classification.title: AN ACT to amend the charter of cities of the fourth class of the Commonwealth of Kentucky.
classification.summary: This act amends section eighty-seven of an act approved June 28, 1893, concerning the government of cities of the fourth class. It allows the board of council to order the construction, reconstruction, or repair of sidewalks costing up to $250. If property owners fail to comply after notice, the city can undertake the improvement and place a lien on the property with 6% annual interest. The contract can be let publicly or privately.
classification.reasoning: The statute concerns municipal improvements, specifically sidewalks, in cities of the fourth class. It outlines procedures for ordering, executing, and financing these improvements, including the city's right to place a lien on abutting property. There is no mention of race, nor are there any provisions that implicitly suggest racial discrimination. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law.

classification.title: An Act to amend an act entitled "An Act to amend and reduce to one the charter of the city of Louisville," approved March 15, 1894, and all acts amendatory thereof, so as to authorize the board of council to provide for the construction, reconstruction and repair of sidewalks.
classification.summary: This act amends the charter of the city of Louisville to allow the board of council to order the construction, reconstruction, or repair of sidewalks. If the cost is under $250, the board can order property owners to make the improvements according to city engineer specifications. If owners fail to comply, the city will make the improvements and place a lien on the property with 6% annual interest. The board has discretion on how to let contracts and whether to require bonds.

classification.title: AN ACT to regulate hunting in the State of Kentucky , requiring certain persons to take out license to hunt .
classification.summary: This act requires non-residents of Kentucky to obtain a hunting license to hunt any protected wild animals, fowls, or birds within the state. The license is valid for one year and is non-transferable. Applicants must fill out a form with their personal information and have it sworn to before an authorized official.
classification.reasoning: The statute requires non-residents to obtain a hunting license. There is no mention of race or any differential treatment based on race. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law. The document type is 'session_laws' as indicated by 'LAWS OF KENTUCKY' and the chapter format. The confidence is high because the statute's intent is clear and does not involve racial discrimination.

classification.title: An Act to Amend Section 1947 of the Kentucky Statutes, Relating to Hunting Licenses
classification.summary: This act amends Kentucky Statutes Section 1947, concerning hunting licenses. It details the requirements for obtaining a license, including a fee of twenty-five dollars and fifty cents, with twenty-five dollars going to the State Treasurer and fifty cents to the clerk. It also specifies that licensees must have their license readily available for inspection and outlines penalties for violations, including fines and potential imprisonment. Furthermore, it mandates monthly reporting by county clerks to the Auditor of State regarding non-resident hunting licenses issued.
classification.reasoning: The statute concerns the regulation of hunting licenses for non-residents in Kentucky. It outlines the application process, fees, requirements for carrying the license, penalties for violations, and reporting procedures for county clerks. There is no mention of race, nor are there any provisions that implicitly suggest racial discrimination. The statute appears to be a regulatory measure for hunting activities and does not fall under the typical categories of Jim Crow laws.

classification.summary: This act amends sections 968, 970, and 971 of the Kentucky Statutes. classification.title: An Act to amend Section 970 of the Kentucky Statutes
classification.summary: This act amends Section 970 of the Kentucky Statutes concerning the appointment and powers of special judges. It modifies the process for selecting a special judge when the initially elected or appointed judge is unable to preside. The amendment allows for agreements between parties, including non-resident defendants and infant defendants (represented by appointed attorneys or guardians ad litem), to select a qualified lawyer to try the case. It also clarifies the procedure for re-selection if the initially agreed-upon person fails to act.
classification.reasoning: The provided text is an amendment to Section 970 of the Kentucky Statutes, which deals with the appointment and powers of special judges in legal proceedings. The amendment focuses on the procedural aspects of selecting a special judge, particularly when parties involved are non-residents or infants. There is no mention of race, nor are there any provisions that implicitly discriminate based on race. Therefore, this statute is not a Jim Crow law. The document type is 'session_laws' as it is presented as part of the 'LAWS OF KENTUCKY' from 1902, indicating it was passed during a legislative session. The confidence is high because the text clearly outlines procedural changes without any racial component.

classification.title: An Act to amend Section 968 of the Kentucky Statutes.
classification.summary: This act amends Section 968 of the Kentucky Statutes concerning the appointment of special judges. It clarifies the process for electing a special judge when the regular judge is absent, unable to preside, or if a party believes they will not receive a fair trial. It also addresses situations involving non-resident or infant defendants.
classification.reasoning: The provided statute text details amendments to sections 968 and 970 of the Kentucky Statutes, focusing on the procedures for appointing special judges in circuit courts. The text outlines conditions under which a special judge can be appointed, such as the regular judge's absence or inability to preside, or if a party files an affidavit for a change of venue due to concerns about a fair trial. It also specifies how special judges are elected or appointed, including provisions for non-resident and infant defendants. There is no mention of race, nor are there any provisions that implicitly suggest racial discrimination. Therefore, this statute is not a Jim Crow law. classification.title: An Act to Regulate the Business of Certain Corporations, Partnerships, Companies, and Associations Engaged in Selling Investment Securities.
classification.summary: This act requires any individual or entity engaged in selling investment securities within Kentucky to deposit a minimum of fifteen thousand dollars in paid-up capital stock or reserve funds with the State Treasurer. These funds are to protect investors. The act also mandates the filing of a financial statement with the Treasurer. It specifies acceptable forms of collateral and investment securities, including cash, bonds, mortgages, and stocks of various corporations, with certain market value requirements.
classification.reasoning: The statute concerns financial regulations for businesses selling investment securities. It mandates capital deposits and financial reporting to protect investors. There is no mention of race, nor are there any provisions that implicitly or explicitly discriminate based on race. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law. The document type is 'session_laws' as it is from the 'LAWS OF KENTUCKY' and refers to the 'passage of this act'.

classification.title: License and Annual Statement Requirements for Corporations
classification.summary: This statute outlines the requirements for corporations, companies, partnerships, or associations to obtain and renew a license to do business in Kentucky. It mandates annual statements detailing business operations, assets, liabilities, premium receipts, and reserve fund allocations. A deposit of $100,000 in cash or securities is required to be built up over time, and upon meeting these requirements, the Treasurer will issue a license that expires annually on January 15th.

classification.title: An Act to amend an act entitled "An act to regulate the business of investment companies organized under the laws of this State, and transacting business in such other State or upon the agents of such investment companies greater than those imposed upon similar companies, by the law of this State, or when such laws of other States shall require investment companies to deposit money or security for the protection of certificateholders of such other State, or shall prohibit companies of this State from transacting business in said State without a special examination of said companies or a computation of their liabilities by the officers of said State, the same taxes, fines, penalties, deposits, and all other requirements shall be imposed upon all investment companies organized in such other State and doing business in this State as are imposed upon such company or corporation by the laws of the State in which said company may be incorporated : And provided further, That where any such corporation,company,partnership or association organized in any other State shall have made a deposit in any other State of the United States for the protection of holders of its certificates, bonds, debentures, certificates of interest or other investment securities, wherever residing, less in amount than that required by the laws of this State, it shall, before doing business in this State, file a certificate of such deposit as herein required, and shall deposit an amount with the Treasurer of this State which, together with the amount so deposited in such other State, shall make up the total amount required by this act to be deposited by said companies in this State; And provided, That when, by the laws of any other State, any such company shall have been required to make, and shall have made, a deposit in such State for the security of holders of its certificates, bonds, debentures, certificates of interest, or other investment securities in such State alone, said company shall not be required to make a deposit in this State of its reserved fund accruing."
classification.summary: This statute outlines reciprocal regulations for investment companies operating across state lines. It establishes that if another state imposes greater taxes, fines, penalties, or deposit requirements on Kentucky investment companies than Kentucky imposes on companies from that state, then Kentucky will impose the same requirements on companies from that other state. It also details provisions for companies that have made deposits in other states for certificate holder protection, requiring them to make up any difference to meet Kentucky's deposit requirements.
classification.reasoning: The statute deals with the regulation of investment companies and their operations across state lines. It focuses on reciprocal taxation, penalties, and deposit requirements between Kentucky and other states. There is no mention of race, and the regulations apply to all investment companies regardless of the race of their organizers or certificate holders. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law.

classification.summary: This act details the election, qualifications, oath, and removal of sinking fund commissioners in cities of the first class. Notably, it specifies that no alderman, councilman, or city, county, or state officer can be a commissioner and must possess qualifications for councilmen in first-class cities. It also establishes a felony charge for embezzlement of sinking fund property or money.

classification.title: AN ACT to amend an act for the government of cities of the fifth class.
classification.summary: This act amends a previous act concerning the government of cities of the fifth class in Kentucky. It re-enacts Section 12 of Article 4 of the Acts of July 3, 1893, to specify the election and terms of city council members, mayor, and police judge, as well as the appointment and terms of the marshal, assessor, treasurer, clerk, and city attorney. The election of city council members, mayor, and police judge is set for the Tuesday after the first Monday in November, with terms beginning on the first Monday in January following the election. The mayor and police judge serve four-year terms, and city council members serve two-year terms. Special provisions are made for the initial terms of police judges elected in November 1893. Appointed officials serve two-year terms and can be removed at the pleasure of the city council.
classification.reasoning: The statute concerns the administrative and electoral structure of cities of the fifth class in Kentucky. It details the election and appointment of city officials and their terms of office. There is no mention of race, nor are there any provisions that implicitly suggest racial discrimination. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law.

classification.title: LAWS OF KENTUCKY
classification.summary: This statute amends sections of a previous act concerning city government. It details the appointment of various city officials by the city council, including a pound-master, superintendent of streets, city engineer, chief of fire department, city weigher, city physician, and police court clerk. It also re-enacts section thirty-seven, placing the department of police under the direction of the city marshal. The marshal is granted powers similar to sheriffs for suppressing riots and disturbances, executing process, prosecuting violations of city ordinances, and collecting taxes. The statute specifies the marshal's duty to pay collected funds to the city treasurer monthly.

There is no mention of race, nor are there any provisions that implicitly suggest racial segregation or discrimination. classification.title: AN ACT to amend sub-section 9 of section 9, of the Acts of June 28, 1893, entitled "An Act for the government of cities of the fourth class."
classification.summary: This act amends a previous law concerning the government of fourth-class cities. Specifically, it modifies sub-section 9 of section 9 of the Acts of June 28, 1893. The amendment pertains to the construction of sewers in these cities. It outlines how the cost of constructing sewers along or under streets, alleys, or highways can be assessed. The cost can be assessed to abutting property owners, not exceeding one dollar per front foot. If the cost exceeds this amount, the entire cost is to be assessed upon lots and lands in the sewer district that benefit from the improvement, with the district being defined before the improvement is made. The council will then fix the tax amount by ordinance.
classification.reasoning: The statute concerns the construction and financing of sewers in fourth-class cities in Kentucky. It details the process of assessing costs to abutting property owners and the establishment of sewer districts. There is no mention of race, nor are there any provisions that implicitly suggest racial discrimination. The focus is purely on municipal infrastructure and property assessment. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law.

CHAPTER 90 .
AN ACT to cure certain defective certificatesof proof deeds , and
to make copies from the records prima facia evidence in the
courts and tribunals of this State .
WHEREAS ,It was heretofore provided by law that "deeds
executed in this State, by persons other than married wo
men , may be admitted to record by proof of two subscrib-
ing witnesses, or by proof of one subscribing witness, who
shall also prove the attestationof the others, and
WHEREAS , Many deeds have beretofore been admitted
to record on proof of one subscribing witness ; and
WHEREAS The clerks,in admitting such deeds to record,
seem generally to have thought itunnecessary to show af-
firmatively in their certificates that such subscribing wit-
ness proved the attestation of the other subscribing wit-
ness. Now , therefore in order to cure all such defective
certificates .
Be itenactedby the General Assembly of the Commonwealth
of Kentucky :
§ 1. Wherever a certificate admitting to record a deed
executed in this State, by a person other than a married
woman , shows that such deed was admitted to record on
the proof of one of the subscribing witnesses thereto, it
shall be presumed that such subscribingwitness proved the
attestation of the other subscribing witness as well as the
execution thereof by the grantor ; and a certifiedcopy of
any such deed which has been,or which may hereafterbe,
recorded in the proper clerk'sofficeshall be prima facie
evidence in all courts and tribunals of this State .
(See Barbour's Code , section fivehundred and one and
section five hundred and nineteen.)
Approved March 24, 1902 .

classification.summary: This act appropriates ten thousand dollars from the state treasury to the Kentucky Children's Home Society for the care, support, maintenance, and provision of homes for homeless and destitute children in the Commonwealth. The appropriation is contingent upon the society executing a bond to ensure the funds are used for their stated purposes. The society must also provide an annual verified statement to the Auditor of Public Accounts detailing the fund's disbursement. Any unexpended funds must be returned to the treasury, and the appropriation must be expended within six years.
classification.reasoning: The statute concerns an appropriation of funds to a children's home society for the care of homeless and destitute children. There is no mention of race, nor are there any provisions that would implicitly or explicitly discriminate based on race. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law. The document type is 'session_laws' as indicated by 'LAWS OF KENTUCKY' and the chapter numbering. The confidence is high because the statute's purpose is clearly defined and has no racial component.

classification.title: AN ACT to provide for stenographic reporters for courts of continuous session.
classification.summary: This act allows judges of circuit courts of continuous session in counties with a population under 150,000, and which constitute a separate judicial district, to appoint a stenographic reporter. The reporter serves for four years or until a successor is appointed, and can be removed for neglect of duty, misbehavior, or incompetence. The reporter must take an oath, attend all sessions unless excused, and take stenographic notes of oral evidence in criminal and penal prosecutions, ordinary actions, and any other proceedings directed by the court. The court can appoint a substitute reporter if the primary reporter is absent or unable to act.
classification.reasoning: The statute concerns the appointment and duties of stenographic reporters in circuit courts of continuous session. It outlines the qualifications, tenure, and responsibilities of these reporters. There is no mention of race, nor are there any provisions that implicitly or explicitly discriminate based on race. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law.

classification.title: Taxation for Redemption of Bonds and Interest
classification.summary: This statute authorizes county courts to levy an annual ad valorem tax on taxable property within a specific part of the county to raise funds for paying interest on and redeeming bonds. The tax is to be collected by the sheriff at the same time as state revenue, with a commission allowed for collection. The tax rate is capped at fifty cents per one hundred dollars of property value. The tax can be levied at any time during the year, and the county judge can convene the court of claims or fiscal court for this purpose.

classification.title: AN ACT to amend an act entitled, "An Act to provide for the creation and regulation of real estate titleinsurance companies."
classification.summary: This act amends Section 9 of Chapter 99 of the Session Acts of 1894, concerning real estate title insurance companies. It changes the permissible percentage of capital stock that can be invested in business-related assets from "twenty" percent to "thirty-three and one-third" percent. The remainder of the stock and accumulations must be invested in bonds and mortgages, lien notes, or deeds of trust on unencumbered real estate within Kentucky, worth at least fifty percent more than the sum loaned.
classification.reasoning: The provided statute text is an amendment to an act concerning the regulation of real estate title insurance companies. It details how these companies can invest their capital stock and accumulations. There is no mention of race, nor are there any provisions that implicitly or explicitly discriminate based on race. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law.

classification.title: AN ACT to regulate the importation into this State of dependent children from other States.
classification.summary: This act regulates the importation of dependent children into Kentucky from other states. It requires individuals, corporations, associations, or institutions to provide a bond of ten thousand dollars to the county judge. This bond ensures that no incorrigible, unsound, or diseased children are brought into the state, and that the Kentucky Children's Home Society is reported to regarding the placement of each child. The bond also covers the cost of returning a child if they become a public charge before the age of twenty-one.

§ 3. At the firstrule day after the summons has been

other plea he may have ,whether of law or of fact.

ply and any other plea he may have whether of law or

court shall consider his excuse insufficient, it may , at the

before the case is called for trial or submitted and the

CHAPTER 125 .
AN ACT to provide for needed improvements at the Branch Peni-
tentiary, Eddyville , Kentucky .
WHEREAS , In the construction of the branch peniten.
tiary it was the intention to erect a cell house on the south
side as well as on the north side of the administration
building, and the building never completed , and a space
leftin the wall where the right cellhouse was to be, said
space now being enclosed by a plank stockade, which is in
a dilapidated and decayed condition; therefore,
Be itenactedby the General Assembly of the Commonwealth
of Kentucky :
§1 . That the commissioners of the Kentucky peniten-
tiariesbe, and hereby are, authorized and instructed to
erect a concrete wall to take the place of the wooden stock
ade .
§ 2. That the sum of six thousand dollars,or so much
thereof as may be necessary, is hereby appropriated
out of the State Treasury for the purpose set forth in the
preceding section of this act .
§ 3. Because of the unsafe condition of the wooden
stockade, and the importance of immediate action in the
premises , an emergency is declared to exist, and this act
shall take effectimmediately upon itspassage .
Became a law without the signature of the Governor March 20 .
1902 .

the first section of the cell house on the south side of and
connecting with the administration building .

ated out of the State Treasury for the purpose set forth

ation . tiary , at Frankfort , Kentucky .
WHEREAS , The chair factory at the Frankfort Peniten-
tiary is heated by exhaust steam from the engines, and
there being sufficientexhaust steam to heat all the other
buildings in that institution where live steam is now
used , said exhaust steam now going to waste , other than
the steam used for the purpose of heating the chair factory,
and ifthe heating apparatuses in the other buildings were
remodeled and put in proper shape and equipped with the
Webster Vacuum system for pumping back the hot con-
densed water for use in the boilers a very large economy
could be effected; therefore ,
Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the Commonwealth
of Kentucky :
§ 1. That the commissioners of the Kentucky peniten-
tiariesbe, and hereby are, authorized and directed to re-
model and put in proper shave the heating apparatuses in
the buildings of the Frankfort Penitentiary, other than
the chair factory, and to purchase and installthe Webster
Vacuum system .
§ 2. That the sum of fifteen thousand dollars, or so
much thereof as may be necessary, is hereby appropri-
ated out of the State Treasury for the purpose set forth in
the preceding section of this act.
§ 3. Because of the great economy set forth in the pre-
ceding section of this act and owing to the improper heat-
ing system in the work shops at the Frankfort Peniten-
tiary, an emergency is declared to exist and this act shall
take effectimmediately upon its passage .
Became a law without the signature of the Governor March 29 ,
1902 .

classification.title: Laws of Kentucky, 1902, Chapter 286
classification.summary: This section of the Laws of Kentucky from 1902 details provisions related to the payment and recovery of taxes on land and personal property. It outlines scenarios where an occupant or tenant pays taxes that the owner should have paid, granting them the right to recover the amount paid plus interest, which then becomes a lien on the property. It also addresses the apportionment of assessments when land owned by multiple individuals is partitioned, and the process for releasing portions of land from delinquent tax liabilities. Furthermore, it establishes a lien for an owner who pays the entire tax on jointly assessed land when other owners are delinquent, allowing them to recover the delinquent's share.

classification.title: LAWS OF KENTUCKY
classification.summary: This excerpt from the Laws of Kentucky of 1902 contains several sections related to taxation and officer compensation. Section 19 prohibits officers from retaining deputy compensation for longer than thirty days, with penalties for violations. Section 20 directs the Auditor to relinquish state claims on lands purchased for taxes assessed prior to 1879. Section 21 outlines the duty of property owners, including those with mineral rights or leases, to list their property for taxation in the county where it is situated, or file a descriptive list with the county clerk, with penalties for willful failure to comply.

classification.title: LAWS OF KENTUCKY
classification.summary: This section of the Laws of Kentucky outlines requirements for property assessment and taxation. It details the information property owners must provide, including the number and value of various livestock (thoroughbred and common stock horses, mules, jacks, jennets, bulls, steers, cows, and calves), bonds, and other taxable estate. It also specifies that errors or informalities in property descriptions or owner names will not invalidate an assessment if the property can be reasonably identified. Furthermore, it mandates that individuals or companies receiving property in pledge for loans must report the value of such pledged property annually for taxation, and they will have a lien on the property to recover taxes paid. The Auditor of Public Accounts is required to provide assessment books and blank schedules to county clerks for assessors.

classification.title: Railroads - chief officer to report to
classification.summary: This statute requires the president or chief officer of any railroad company operating in Kentucky to report annually to the Auditor of Public Accounts. The report must include the total length of the railroad, its length within the state and in each county, city, incorporated town, and taxing district, along with the average value per mile. It also requires a list of depot grounds, improvements, and other real estate owned by the company and their values. For companies operating railroads outside of Kentucky, a proportion of the rolling stock value is to be reported based on the ratio of in-state mileage to total mileage. Failure to file the report by the specified date subjects the chief officer to a fine.

classification.title: LAWS OF KENTUCKY
classification.summary: This statute outlines the process for collecting and distributing school taxes levied on railroad companies. Specifically, it mandates that taxes levied in common school districts be paid to the county superintendent for the benefit of the district. Crucially, it states that the provisions of this law do not apply to colored school districts. However, it then details a distribution mechanism where railroad taxes collected in white graded or common school districts are to be apportioned between those white districts and any colored common school districts located within the same boundary. The distribution ratio is based on the number of white children of pupil age and colored children of pupil age residing in the district.
classification.reasoning: The statute explicitly mentions 'colored school district' and establishes a system for distributing school taxes that differentiates between white and colored school districts. Section 6 states that the law 'shall not be construed to apply to any colored school district' but then proceeds to detail a distribution of funds between white and colored districts based on pupil demographics. This clearly indicates a racially segregated system for educational funding, a hallmark of Jim Crow laws. The document type is inferred as 'session_laws' from the header 'LAWS OF KENTUCKY' and the page numbering indicating it's part of a legislative compilation. The confidence is high because of the explicit mention of racial categories in the context of school funding distribution.

classification.title: An Act to amend Section 4076 of the Kentucky Statutes, relating to the assessment of property.
classification.summary: This act amends Kentucky Statutes Section 4076 concerning the appointment and duties of tax supervisors. It outlines the process for appointing additional supervisors from different wards in cities of the third or fourth class, the delivery of appointment orders by the county clerk to the sheriff, and the oath required of supervisors. The supervisors are tasked with assessing property at its fair cash value and are subject to fines for unexcused absence. They convene annually on the first Monday of January to review the assessor's book and other information to determine property values.

classification.title: LAWS OF KENTUCKY
classification.summary: This section of the Laws of Kentucky from 1902 details regulations concerning the issuance and transferability of licenses. It specifies that licenses must be on official blanks, valid for no more than one year, and generally non-transferable. classification.title: An Act to Amend and Re-enact Section 37 of Chapter 105 of the Kentucky Statutes, Relating to Peddlers
classification.summary: This statute defines who is considered a peddler, including those who operate under the guise of bona fide merchants to sell goods temporarily in any county, city, or town. It requires applicants for peddler licenses to disclose their name, residence, ownership of goods, and any interested parties. It also outlines specific requirements for sales advertised as insurance, bankrupt, assignee, or closing-out sales, or sales of damaged goods, mandating affidavits and court examination. False statements are punishable as false swearing.
classification.reasoning: The statute concerns the regulation of peddlers and merchants, requiring licenses and disclosures for sales. There is no mention of race, nor any provisions that implicitly target or discriminate against any racial group. The focus is purely on commercial regulation and consumer protection through disclosure requirements. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law.

classification.title: An Act relating to insurance premiums and taxes
classification.summary: This statute requires local resident agents of insurance companies to file a sworn statement of premiums received and losses paid for policies written in Kentucky. It also mandates a tax of two dollars per one hundred dollars of premiums received. A similar detailed statement and tax are required for policies covering property located in the state, regardless of where they were written (home office, branch office, broker, non-resident agent, or re-insurance). Failure to comply within thirty days incurs a penalty. Companies failing to make these reports are guilty of a misdemeanor and subject to a fine. The statute also addresses mutual assessment companies not organized under Kentucky law.
classification.reasoning: The statute concerns the regulation of insurance companies, specifically focusing on the reporting of premiums, payment of taxes, and penalties for non-compliance. There is no mention of race, nor are there any provisions that implicitly suggest racial discrimination. The requirements apply to all insurance companies operating within Kentucky, regardless of the race of the policyholders or agents. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law. The document type is 'session_laws' as it is from the 'LAWS OF KENTUCKY' and dated '1902'.

No. 12. *
RESOLUTION relative to the charges against Admiral Winfield
Scott Schley .
WHEREAS , Admiral Winfield Scott Schley, the hero of
Santiago,the savior of the ill-fated Greeley expedition, and
America's greatest and most popular naval officer,has been
slandered and grossly mistreated by what is termed the
naval clique, in a manner which is now known of allintel.
ligentpeople ; therefore,
Be itresolvedby the General Assembly of the Commonwealth
of Kentucky :
§ 1. That we deprecate a condition of affairs in the Unit-
ed States navy that willpermit or condone such unjust and
unwarranted attacks upon a brave and unflinching officer
who so ably conducted in person the Santiago naval battle
and sent the Spanish ships to Davy Jones ' locker.
§ 2. That we , the representatives and senators of Ken-
tucky, call upon our representatives in Congress to use
every effortto make due reparation officiallyin compliance
with the popular verdict of the American people already
rendered .
§ 3. That having information that Admiral Schley will
soon visitthe city of Louisville, Resolved, That this Legis-
lature do invite him to address it in the House of Repre-
sentatives at that time .

ernor .
